narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, you presented a profile with some promising elements, including middle-class family income and plans to attend full-time. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 82% chance that you would fail it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this model to prioritize students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate successfully. The decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, specifically your position in Year 3, which had the strongest pull toward predicting failure. Your Year 1 decile ranking reinforced this concern, as both placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. These low rankings signaled to the model a pattern of underperformance that strongly outweighed other factors for someone in your demographic. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA pushed the prediction slightly in the direction of passing, acting as a counterbalance since it's right around the average of 3.21 for admitted students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your combination of low school deciles stood out as a major red flag, even with your LSAT close to the average of 36.7. While full-time status is common and didn't sway things much, the model saw your early grades as the biggest hurdles. To move forward, focus on strengthening your academic record—perhaps retake courses or pursue targeted prep to boost those decile rankings, as schools value demonstrated improvement. Consider retaking the LSAT to exceed the average, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a male applicant from a middle-income background. With targeted efforts, you can reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's view in your favor.
